title: AI-assisted coding interview format: 20min live session with coding agent
description:
## Concept

An interview format where the candidate gets 20 minutes with a coding agent (e.g., Claude Code) while the interviewer observes.

## What It Evaluates

| Skill | How observed |
| ----- | ------------ |
| Agent fluency | How naturally they interact with the AI — prompting, correcting, steering |
| Tool efficiency | Keyboard shortcuts, command usage, workflow speed |
| Judgment | When they override the agent vs. trust it; what they verify |
| Innovation | Creative use of agent capabilities; novel approaches to the problem |
| Communication | How they explain their thinking while working |
| Code literacy | Can they read and evaluate AI-generated code quickly? |

## Why This Matters

Traditional coding interviews test memorized algorithms. This format tests **how effectively someone works with AI tools** — which is the actual job now.

## Open Questions

- What task to give? Open-ended feature? Bug fix? Refactor?
- Which agent/tool to standardize on, or let candidate choose?
- Scoring rubric — how to compare candidates fairly?
- Does this disadvantage candidates unfamiliar with a specific agent?
- Should the candidate explain as they go, or review at the end?
notes:
## Additional Evaluation Criteria: Work Habits

Beyond coding skill, this format reveals meta-skills invisible in traditional interviews:

| Habit | What to observe |
| ----- | --------------- |
| Scope awareness | When they discover the task is part of a bigger issue, do they note it and stay focused, or rabbit-hole? |
| Scalable planning | Do they break work into steps before coding, or dive in? |
| Reaction to surprise | How they handle unexpected findings (bug leads to design flaw, etc.) |
| Communication instinct | Do they explain what they found, or silently context-switch? |
| Tool discipline | Do they commit incrementally, leave notes, track loose ends? |

This may be more valuable than the coding itself — a normal coding session tests "can you solve X" while this tests "how do you work."

title: Unified plugin system — combine rules, skills, and CLI for agent harnesses
description: Current Claude Code plugin management is fragmented: rules, skills, MCP, CLI, hooks are all separate. Cannot combine rules with skills, cannot enable/disable individual rules easily. Agent console resolves this by: (1) combining rules + skills + CLI into one package, (2) offering freely customizable UI/TUI for extension creation, (3) dynamic injection per session, (4) adapter layer for Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI. MCP should retire — skills with CLI can do everything MCP does. Related: .git-globally-ignored/drafts/plugin-extension-system.md

title: AI-powered chip die shot analyzer: image to abstract block diagram
description:
## Concept

Software that takes a chip die shot (photograph/microscope image of a CPU/GPU die) and produces an abstracted block diagram showing functional regions.

### Input

- Die shot image (microscope photo of a silicon chip)
- Could also be circuit board images

### Output

- Abstracted map/diagram identifying functional blocks (CPU cores, GPU cores, cache, memory controller, I/O, etc.)
- Interactive: user can point to a region and ask "what is this?" and get an educated guess

### Inspiration

- Human experts (not necessarily top-tier) can visually identify functional regions on die shots by recognizing patterns (regular arrays = cache/memory, repeated blocks = cores, etc.)
- Goal: automate this recognition process
- Reference: https://youtu.be/kXX9n62N72s?t=378

### Key Features

- Image → abstract block diagram conversion
- Interactive Q&A about specific regions
- Pattern recognition for common structures (SRAM arrays, logic blocks, I/O pads, etc.)

## Open Questions

- Training data: are labeled die shots available publicly?
- Scope: just identification, or also reverse engineering functionality?
- Target users: hardware enthusiasts, journalists, competitive analysis teams?
- How detailed should the output be? (top-level blocks vs. sub-block detail)

## Status

Idea phase — needs further review and detail from creator.

title: LLM plays games — auto-play Wordle, puzzles, word games
description: LLM agents that actually play games like Wordle, puzzles, word games, etc. Different from fi-46 (daily login automation) — this is about the agent understanding and playing the game itself.

title: Skills with CLI will replace MCP
description: Idea originated in 2025 when skills rolled out for Claude Code. Formalized when skills became an open standard. Core thesis: skills + CLI can do everything MCP servers do, but with better ergonomics — no separate server process, no transport layer, just callable tools. MCP adds unnecessary complexity for most use cases. Skills are more composable, easier to distribute, and already integrated into the agent harness.
